courthouse.name: Osage County Courthouse
courthouse.hours: Monday-Friday, 8:30 AM-5:00 PM (closed 12:00-1:00 PM for lunch)

majorEmployers.name: Pioneer Woman Mercantile

uniqueFacts: Osage County is the largest county by area in Oklahoma, covering 2,246 square miles, and is coextensive with the Osage Nation Reservation
The county was the site of the 'Reign of Terror' in the 1920s, depicted in the book and film 'Killers of the Flower Moon,' where Osage tribal members were murdered for their oil wealth
The Osage Nation is the only tribe in Oklahoma to retain a federally recognized reservation that covers an entire county
The Pioneer Woman Mercantile in Pawhuska, owned by Food Network star Ree Drummond, draws tourists from across the country and has revitalized the county seat's economy
The Tallgrass Prairie Preserve in northern Osage County is the largest protected tallgrass prairie remnant in North America, spanning nearly 40,000 acres
processServingNotes: Osage County is entirely within the Osage Nation Reservation; process servers should be aware of tribal jurisdiction considerations when serving on tribal members or tribal trust land

The Osage County Courthouse is located at 600 Grandview Avenue in Pawhuska, with the Court Clerk's office in Room 304. The courthouse is open Monday through Friday, 8:30 AM to 5:00 PM, closed for lunch from 12:00-1:00 PM
Court records can be searched online through OSCN (osage) or ODCR (court code 036). In-person records requests can be made at the Court Clerk's office at (918) 287-4104
heroLead: Osage County is Oklahoma's largest county at 2,246 square miles, entirely coextensive with the Osage Nation Reservation and rich with Native American heritage, oil history, and the tourism buzz from Ree Drummond's Pioneer Woman Mercantile in Pawhuska. For reliable process serving throughout this unique jurisdiction, contact (539) 367-6832 or visit /contact.

faqs.question: Where is the Osage County Courthouse located?
faqs.answer: The Osage County Courthouse is located at 600 Grandview Avenue, Pawhuska, OK 74056, and has been serving the county since its construction in 1912. The courthouse sits on a hill overlooking downtown Pawhuska. Court Clerk Lavendar Carroll's office is in Room 304, reachable at 918-287-4104.

faqs.question: What makes Osage County unique in Oklahoma?
faqs.answer: Osage County is unique because it is Oklahoma's largest county by area at 2,246 square miles, and the entire county is coextensive with the Osage Nation Reservation. The Osage Nation is the only tribe in Oklahoma with a reservation that covers an entire county. For process serving assistance in this unique jurisdiction, call (539) 367-6832.

faqs.question: What are the courthouse hours for filing legal documents?
faqs.answer: The Osage County Court Clerk's office is open Monday through Friday, 8:30 AM to 5:00 PM, with lunch closure from 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM. The office accepts filings for civil, criminal, probate, juvenile, domestic relations, and small claims cases. Payments for fines and court costs can also be made online at www.odcr.com.

faqs.question: How do I search court records for Osage County?
faqs.answer: Osage County court records can be searched online through the Oklahoma State Courts Network (OSCN) using the county dbkey 'osage', or through On-Demand Court Records (ODCR) using court code 036. For certified copies or in-person record searches, contact the Court Clerk's office at 918-287-4104 at the Osage County Courthouse in Pawhuska.

faqs.question: What towns and cities are in Osage County?
faqs.answer: Osage County includes 13 incorporated towns and cities: Pawhuska (county seat), Hominy, Skiatook, Barnsdall, Fairfax, Shidler, Avant, Burbank, Wynona, Grainola, Osage, Prue, and Foraker. Several larger cities including Tulsa, Bartlesville, and Sand Springs extend partially into Osage County. The county covers the entire Osage Nation Reservation.

faqs.question: What is the judicial district for Osage County?
faqs.answer: Osage County is part of the 10th Judicial District of Oklahoma. The county has three judges: one District Judge, one Associate District Judge, and one Special Judge. All civil, criminal, family, and probate cases for Osage County are heard at the Osage County Courthouse in Pawhuska. For process serving needs in the 10th Judicial District, contact (539) 367-6832.
